one_line_takeaway: EARBench is the first automated benchmark showing that foundation-model task planners for embodied agents almost always (avg TRR 95.75%) produce physically unsafe plans, and even explicit-safety prompting leaves the best model above 40% risk.
key_finding: All 12 evaluated models show alarmingly high physical-risk rates: average TRR 95.75%, with even the best model (Gemini 1.5 Flash) at 93.67% and GPT-4o at 94.03%, while TER stays high (~80-95%, GPT-4o 96.53%) — models generate executable plans but ignore safety. Scaling parameters helps only marginally and inconsistently (Llama 3.1 8B 96.93% vs 70B 95.51%; DeepSeek-V2 at 236B is an exception at 96.7%). Visual input gives only a slight edge over text (GPT-4o 94.53%->93.52%). Explicit safety tips in the evaluator raise human-agreement Kappa from 0.48 to 0.85. Mitigation prompts help but are insufficient: RM-Explicit (avg ~14% improvement) drops GPT-4o to 45.64% and Llama 3.1-70B to 67.85%, yet even the best model stays above 40% TRR.
core_contribution: EARBench is presented as the first automated framework for assessing physical-risk awareness of foundation-model-based embodied AI (EAI) agents used as high-level task planners. Its own contributions are: (1) a four-module multi-agent pipeline (Safety Guidelines Generation, Risky Scene Generation, Embodied Task Planning, Plan Assessment) that generates test cases and evaluates plans end-to-end; (2) EARDataset, 2,636 test cases spanning 28 scenes across 7 domains, split evenly into 1,318 textual and 1,318 visual scenarios; (3) two evaluation metrics, Task Risk Rate (TRR, fraction of plans containing a physical risk) and Task Effectiveness Rate (TER, fraction of executable plans); (4) a large-scale evaluation of 12 open- and closed-source LLMs/VLMs; and (5) two prompt-based mitigation strategies (RM-Implicit, RM-Explicit). The 'Constitutional AI' concept (Bai et al. [24]) is cited inspiration, not a contribution of this paper; GPT-4o and Midjourney-V6 are used as generation/evaluation tools, not proposed here.
threat_model: This is a safety/robustness evaluation, not an adversarial-attack paper. The hazard studied is a foundation-model 'brain' producing physically dangerous high-level task plans when an embodied agent faces a real-world scene plus a risk-inducing (but not maliciously crafted) natural-language instruction — e.g., placing a metal/aluminum-foil item in a microwave (fire), disposing of biohazardous syringes in regular trash (contamination), or welding near flammable pallets (fire). The 'attacker surface' is the instruction+scene that induces the agent to execute an unsafe step; the failure is the agent's lack of risk awareness rather than a jailbreak or data-poisoning adversary. The paper does not invent a malicious adversary and I do not impose one.
limitations: TRR/TER and the entire dataset are generated and judged largely by GPT-4o, creating a self-referential loop where the same model family both authors risk-inducing cases and scores them (partially mitigated but not eliminated by human validation on only 200 of 2,636 cases). Evaluation stays at high-level plan text — no physical simulation, real robot, or low-level execution confirms that a flagged plan would actually cause harm or that a 'safe' plan is truly safe. The multimodal VLM set is small (GPT-4o, GPT-4o mini, Claude 3 Haiku, Gemini 1.5 Flash), and closed models are older tiers (GPT-3.5 Turbo, Claude 3 Haiku), so results may not reflect current frontier models. The binary risk indicator and single-tip-per-case design may over-count risk (a plan is 'unsafe' if it contains the one targeted risk step, e.g. a correct warning placed after a dangerous step is still scored unsafe).

datasets_used: EARDataset (introduced by this paper): 2,636 test cases, 28 scenes across 7 domains; 1,318 textual + 1,318 visual scenarios
Domain distribution: Home 38.3% (838), Commercial 23.0% (504), Medical 10.8% (236), Science 10.4% (228), Industrial 7.8% (170), Education 6.8% (148), Entertainment 2.9% (64)
Human validation subset: 200 randomly selected instances, each labeled by 5 annotators
quantitative_results: Best model TRR: Gemini 1.5 Flash 93.67%; GPT-4o TRR 94.03%; Claude 3 (Haiku) 95.05%
Open-source avg TRR 96.38% vs closed-source avg TRR 94.86%
Model-size effect: Llama 3.1-8B 96.93% vs 70B 95.51%; Qwen2-7B 96.59% vs 72B 95.56%; Mixtral-8x7B 97.03% vs 8x22B 96.33%; DeepSeek-V2 (236B) 96.7% (exception)
Textual vs visual TRR: Gemini 1.5 Flash 94.46%->93.06%; GPT-4o 94.53%->93.52%
Automated-vs-human agreement (Cohen's Kappa): 0.48 without safety tips -> 0.85 with safety tips

Mitigation: RM-Implicit avg ~4% improvement, RM-Explicit avg ~14%; RM-Explicit drops GPT-4o to 45.64%, Llama 3.1-70B 95.51%->67.85% (implicit only ->90.57%), Claude 3 Haiku 95.12%->66.34%; best model still >40% TRR
baselines_compared: Text-only LLMs: GPT-3.5 Turbo, Llama-3.1 8B, Llama-3.1 70B, Qwen2 7B, Qwen2 72B, DeepSeek-V2 (236B), Mixtral 8x7B, Mixtral 8x22B
Multimodal VLMs: GPT-4o, GPT-4o mini, Claude 3 Haiku, Gemini 1.5 Flash
Prompting-strategy comparison: no-mitigation vs RM-Implicit vs RM-Explicit
